repo.description: The most widely used Python to C compiler
repo.name: cython

body:
Fixes #7742.

Also work around a bug in Python where the overflow check meant we didn't set the thread id to unowned.

Also use `PyUnstable_SetImmortal` on Python 3.15+ instead.

comment_data.body:
Since we should only reach this code in CPython 3.14, can't we just call `_Py_SetImmortal()` there, like `pythoncapi_compat.h` does?
https://github.com/python/pythoncapi-compat/blob/75a796764d63327268d195e2d5c044e564d0dada/pythoncapi_compat.h#L2692-L2693
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -2516,19 +2516,26 @@ def immortalize_constants(array_cname, constant_count, writer):
         writer.putln("{")
         writer.putln(f"PyObject **table = {array_cname};")
         writer.putln(f"for (Py_ssize_t i=0; i<{constant_count}; ++i) {{")
-        writer.putln("#if CYTHON_COMPILING_IN_CPYTHON_FREETHREADING")
+        writer.putln("#if PY_VERSION_HEX >= 0x030F0000")
+        writer.putln("PyUnstable_SetImmortal(table[i]);")
+        writer.putln("#elif CYTHON_COMPILING_IN_CPYTHON_FREETHREADING")
         # We don't want to set the refcount on shared constants (e.g. cached integers)
         # because setting the refcount isn't thread-safe. The chances are that most of the constants
         # that this applies to are already immortal though so that isn't a great loss.
+        # Overflow, e.g. on 32 bit systems.
+        writer.putln("if ((PY_SSIZE_T_MAX <= _Py_IMMORTAL_REFCNT_LOCAL)) break;")
         writer.putln("#if PY_VERSION_HEX < 0x030E0000")
         writer.putln("if (_Py_IsOwnedByCurrentThread(table[i]) && Py_REFCNT(table[i]) == 1)")
         writer.putln("#else")
         writer.putln("if (PyUnstable_Object_IsUniquelyReferenced(table[i]))")
         writer.putln("#endif")
         writer.putln("{")
-        writer.putln("Py_SET_REFCNT(table[i], _Py_IMMORTAL_REFCNT_LOCAL);")
+        # Go one higher than we think we need to because of a bug in SET_REFCNT check in CPython
+        writer.putln("Py_SET_REFCNT(table[i], ((Py_ssize_t)_Py_IMMORTAL_REFCNT_LOCAL + 1));")

comment_data.body:
I think this covers 3.13t as well (although it's possibly debatable what the real value is in supporting that).

It also needs an internal include to access, although obviously that's something we could do.

comment_data.body:
My guess is that the traction of the latest Py3.1?t improvements is still high enough for most users to keep upgrading quickly, so Py3.14t is probably the minimum for production currently.

Anyway, I was just throwing in the question. It's clean for Py3.15 now and if this change gets it to work for Py3.13/3.14, that's fine with me.

comment_data.body:
> My guess is that the traction of the latest Py3.1?t improvements is still high enough for most users to keep upgrading quickly, so Py3.14t is probably the minimum for production currently.

I think a lot of people have taken that view, and have decided to drop support for 3.13t pretty much as soon as 3.14t was released. I'm not sure we want to be quite that strict but it probably has few users and we shouldn't support it for too long.

comment_data.body:
> It also needs an internal include to access, although obviously that's something we could do.

FWIW, the way I think about this is despite the use of internals, the defintions in the pythoncapi_compat are to some extent "officially" blessed as the correct translations of modern idioms to older versions of the C API. So copying what the compat headers do almost completely mitigates use of internals, in my mind.

commit.message:
Fix 32 bit immortalization (by turning it off)

Fixes #7742.

Also work around a bug in Python where the overflow check meant
we didn't set the thread id to unowned.

Also use `PyUnstable_SetImmortal` on Python 3.15+ instead.

title: Fix 32 bit immortalization (by turning it off)
